item.title: My sweet ex-husband is dying tomorrow
item.description:
This is Shawn. Shawn and I were married from 2014-2020. He was an amazing father to our daughter, and an even more amazing Papa Bear to my two older daughters.
Shawn was so kind. He was the kindest, most gentle, most generous man I had ever met. I had to marry him.
I loved doing life with him, but ultimately we were better together as friends than as spouses. He LOVED being a father.
On January 30 he had a major stroke and went to the hospital. Overnight he had a second major stroke that put him in a coma. The neurologist was not sure if or when he would come to, but let us know his life on the other side of this coma was extremely poor quality. He would be blind, have paralysis in his limbs, may not know who we are, would need a feeding tube, and be kept in a nursing home indefinitely. His mother made the hard choice to bring hospice in after he spent 2 weeks in a coma.
I want to remember him as the father he was. He was so loving and so proud of his daughters. He loved his family and friends with all his heart. I'm so proud to have known him and shared 8 years of my life with him. I love you, Shawn Paul.
item.dc:creator: chickensareawesome

item.title: Train Jane
item.description:
Radio presenter Greg James made a new friend on the train today
Best buds!
item.dc:creator: mynameisnotphoebe

item.title: "Stuck in the Back"
item.description: I did not write these tweets, but according to the twitter bio of the guy who did, he "Was not stuck in a hotel" and "wrote the story for fun". I don't mind, though. It elicited a genuine anxiety response in me. Especially photos of the dark hallways and the single stack of chairs.
item.dc:creator: tehpipe

item.title: Day 261: of cheeseburgers I want inside of me
item.description: Oklahoma John Double Smash Patties, Fried Onion, Single American Cheese, Deep Fried Jalapenos, Maple Bacon, In-house Chili Sauce On A Potato Bun From John Belvedere In Parramatta NSW, Australia Previous Day 260: https://imgur.com/gallery/8z2f7p8 MESSAGE ME BURGER PICS OR YOUR FAVORITE BURGER SPOTS! Map of all burger spots that are available on yelp! Big thanks to @OhBoyDoIHaveNews4U for this. Link to his post and map https://imgur.com/gallery/vcObDuD

item.title: Border Collie herding ducklings to water
item.dc:creator: SteveHarveysTalkingMustache

item.title: I'm still buzzed from the halftime show! WEST SIDE!
item.description:
I'm 38, and my 10yr old son definitely enjoyed watching his mom and dad jamming tf out
Just GOAT things
Hip Hop royalty
Accurate.
So everybody, just follow me! Cause we need a little controversy, cause it feels so empty WITHOUT ME
No hate on 50, a little lighthearted jokes but it's all in good fun. Full disclosure, I weigh 45lbs more than I did 10yrs ago
Snoop can pull off anything lol
For real tho, god DAMN Mary J!

1. #mindful_parenting
2. #theoggentleparent
3. I originally heard this said on the show Louie
4. #empathy
5. ❤️
6. #anxiety
7. Love this
8. #control
9. A good reminder
10. Raising strong daughters
11. Makes sense
12. ?
13. My bonus daughter would totally do something like this ?
14. But really though
15. ??‍♀️
16. ??‍♀️
17. On days that seem extra tough I try to remember this
18. ?
19. Haha fork knife #fortnite
20. The wooorrrsssttt
21. ?
22. ??????
23. The struggle is real when you have ADHD and are trying so hard to actively listen to your very excited kiddo’s never ending story #adhd
24. My bonus son loves to collect things
25. ?
26. Had this happen last year
27. My husband and I are so fortunate to have this type of relationship with his ex and her husband! Successful co-parenting
28. Ope
29. I like these
30. Haha yep
31. ❤️
32. ☀️
33. Daily reminder
34. ?
35. We’re all assholes sometimes and it’s okay to recognize that
36. ?
37. Happened more than once for sure
38.
39. Some days I do a better job at this than others ❤️
40. #mental_health
41. ?
42. Mr. Rogers was so great!
43. ❤️
44. ?
45. Love this so much
46. ??‍♀️
47. ?
48. The only way that we are on time to school every day is by getting there 20 minutes early ?
49. ?

item.title: 59 year old Ukrainian grandmother receiving combat training in preparation for Russian invasion
item.description:
Source: https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/world-news/gran-59-trains-army-ukraine-26149027
Civilian volunteers train for war with dummy guns near Kiev.
Volunteers training with Ukraine's Territorial Defence Forces include Yuri, a 12 year old boy.
item.dc:creator: PrincePiggo

item.title: Life after death
item.description:
Ernest Kreiling (1923-2008) served in "I Company" United States 114th Infantry Regiment from 1941-45, he reached the rank of staff sergeant and earned a Bronze Star. He met Jean Vonachen in Junior High School. They were married on July 31, 1945. In 1947, while studying at the University of Zurich, Switzerland, Ernest returned to North-West Europe with his new wife and LIFE photographer Anthony Linck (1919-2004) as Ernest retraced his wartime steps through France and Germany, giving an intimate view of his experiences as a soldier to his wife and providing us today with an astonishingly stark and stunning look at the aftermath of the Second World War in western Europe. I have some details from the captions presented in "Life" in 1947, but not many, as very few of these images were printed in "Life", so have had to do some of my own research, use the comments of more-informed people than me, and make some assumptions in the captions.
The couple approach the Fort de Manonviller; today the whole area is very thickly forested. This position was part of the 114th Infantry Regiment's first deployment to the front line in Europe on October 18th 1944. Constructed in 1882 to defend France, this fort had been captured by the Germans in 1914 during World War One; after recapture later that year it does not seem to have played host to any major events, in 1935 it was sold to a private citizen (presumably a farmer of the area) and during the occupation of France in World War Two the Germans took some steps to further reduce it as a viable military position. "Cannon Company" of the 114th fired the 114th's first shots of the war from around this area, into German positions in the village of Veho about two and a half miles to the east.
The Fort de Manonviller is in roughly the centre of this very fine map from the 114th's war diary.
Ernest and Jean cycle by a flipped German Panzer IV. Unusually, this looks like one that may have been left in this position due to enemy action - potentially an airstrike, judging by what looks like the main hull having been buckled. It is also possible though that it was simply dragged off the road - the "buckling" may be a part of the armour protection designed to lever away for maintenance hanging loose. The reason for so many tanks being upside down is seldom to do with the violence of action - disabled vehicles that were beyond recovery were often stripped of useful parts, and turning a tank over to strip things like road wheels is easier than carefully jacking one up.
The pair talk to French farmers, continuing their lives in the liberated ruins. This may be in Enchenberg (Moselle, Lorraine) where Mr Kreiling was able to rediscover and have dinner with a French family who first met him during the war.
Mr and Mrs Kreiling examine one of those 'small' bits of cosmetic damage war leaves which could, for those there when it occurred, have been their defining, or even final, moments.
Mrs Kreiling talks to some French children; Mr Kreiling may well be thinking that some of these children would've been just babes-in-arms when he and his colleagues wandered through the area with guns; pock-marking this very building. The 114th war diary noted that on 8th December 1944, while fighting in Enchenberg (Moselle, Lorraine), street and house fighting was so close that American troops clearly heard German soldiers speaking between exchanges of fire and grenades, with mortars at times needing to fire from one side of a building to the other. The "mousehole" patched up with straw and sheets to the left of the picture also has the look not of battle damage, but deliberate creation - potentially to site a machine-gun or anti-tank gun position in the house.
Mr and Mrs Kreiling amidst the lingering desolation of a war now over. The vehicle is a German Panther tank (Panzer V). This is possibly part of the vast German fortifications called the "Siegfried Line". The damage to the house has the tell-tale marks of "direct fire" damage - shells from artillery or armour being fired at relatively close range straight through it.
The same image from a differing angle, the married couple walking hand and hand away from the destruction, the abandoned Panther tank, and the fields of wire toward their future together.
The couple examine an Allied Sherman tank; as discussed above, it has likely been flipped to aid salvage - note how many of the wheels are missing. There appear to be other disabled tanks in the distance. The bottom of the tank (facing the sky) says "vendu" - "sold", indicating a scrap dealer already has dibs on this wreck.
Mr and Mrs Kreiling examine what appears to be the wreck of a P-51 Mustang fighter. Mr Kreiling is holding the receiver of one of the plane's M2 Browning machine guns.
The pair examine a long-abandoned fox hole - complete with a US army ration pack still within. It's interesting to note that in all these pictures Mr Kreiling is still wearing a M43 US army issue jacket, potentially the very one he wore in these battlefields. The ration pack is one half of a "Ration, Ten in One", a meal set designed to feed ten soldiers for one day, which was issued from 1943. It used a simplified form of the standard US "K-ration" packaging, and had five menus for variety. "Menu 1", as seen here, contained a package of hard candy as its unique offering.
Mrs Kreiling evidently quite likes the way her husband looks when protected by the M1 helmet (which was the standard US helmet from 1941-1985). The original "Life" magazine article credits this defensive position as the first place Mr Kreiling ever heard the whistle of incoming enemy artillery fire. My own research suggests to me these positions may be in the vicinity of Saverne (Bas-Rhin).
Examining what appears to be a small machine gun nest. Mr Kreiling is also wearing what appear to be wartime issue "buckle boots" on this trip. It is possible these defensive positions are those held by G and F company of the 114th around November 25th 1944, in which the regiment was attacked by a strong combined-arms force of the 901st Panzergrenadiers and the 130th Panzer regiment (both constituent parts of the Panzer Lehr Division). The 114th fought at close range with the enemy panzers (Lehr committed 34 Panzer IVs and 38 Panthers to this attack), which moved into their defensive positions almost at will, but held them off - saving the flank of the 7th Army and earning the 2nd Battalion of the 114th a Presidential Unit Citation. The damage done to Panzer Lehr Division by the 114th likely delayed the launch of Operation Wacht am Rhein - the Battle of the Bulge.
Mr and Mrs Kreiling bed down in a barn in which Mr Krieling had slept during the war at Lixheim (Moselle, Lorraine) - to be woken by a German counter-attack.
The pair and a heavily damaged jeep. The original "Life" article tells us that Mr Kreiling watched this jeep be loaded with a man wounded by a German sniper, only for the jeep to run over a mine as it tried to leave the area.
Mr Kreiling examines a heavily-damaged US helmet near the destroyed jeep. This picture has, I think, captured a sense of the "there for the grace of god go I" aspect of surviving a war - as the classics say, we are all ignorant of the God's plans for us.
The Kreilings examine a destroyed German tank-destroyer - a Sturmgeschutz III ("Stug"), Germany's most-produced armoured fighting vehicle of the Second World War. There are two or three German Stalhelm helmets piled up on the track guard furthest from us.
Another look at the disabled Stug.
The couple stand on a destroyed road bridge crossing the railway line near Avricourt train station (today the bridge has been rebuilt and this crossing links the D7 and D40 county roads). It was around this area on November 15th 1944 that I Company of the 114th, reinforced with additional machine guns and heavy mortars - Mr Kreiling's company - fought at the 'tip of the spear' in an attack credited with opening a hole in German lines through which the entire 7th Army poured towards the Vosges Mountains and eventually the liberation of both Sarrebourg and Strasbourg.
This is, I think, the Eglise St Denis; at the eastern end of the French town of Sarreguemines. Its spire has been replaced with a more angular number. The 17th SS Panzergrenadier Division "Götz von Berlichingen" fought Mr Kreiling here during Operation Nordwind (December '44 - Jan '45). He and the other American soldiers with him were not found wanting. Despite the reinforcement of the 17th SS Panzergrenadiers with additional Panther tanks, some 21st Panzer division Flammpanzer 38(t)s (flame-thrower armed tanks) and support from the 653rd Heavy Panzerjager's mighty Jadgtiger heavy tank-destroyers; the Americans held and the 17th SS divisional commanding officer SS-Standartenführer Hans Linger was captured by a patrol of the 114th.
The Eglise St Denis is roughly at the where the "A" of what I assume is "A Company"'s position in Neunkirchen. Today the town of Sarreguemines has expanded and taken in Neunkirchen. Operation Nordwind was the last major offensive by Nazi Germany on the western front. The 17th SS Panzergrenadiers, having been repelled by Mr Krieling, would go on to gain infamy with an attempt to use Allied POWs held at Stalag VII-A (Moosberg) as hostages to cover their retreat in April 1945; and the fanatical units final action of the war was a battle at Castle Itter (Tyrol) on 5 May 1945 - in which the SS fought against both the American & German Armies, while trying to murder an array of high-ranking, mainly French, prisoners held at the Castle.
A lonely German grave by the roadside, somewhere in France or Germany. This picture reminds me very strongly of what I regard as one of most tragic sets of war graves in Germany. Outside of Roth, Bavaria, almost hidden alongside the north-south road as it climbs to the south end of town, lie four graves - one forty-one year old (Wilhelm Richter), two seventeen year olds (Kurt-Eberhard Nonne & Harald Pickert), and one nineteen year old (Christian Maracotti (sp?)). German soldiers of the 20th Cavalry Replacement Battalion, they were killed while trying to ambush American forces on April 8 1945. A month later, and those three teenagers would've had their whole lives back.
This is today the Lorraine American Cemetery and Memorial, St Avold. Ten thousand four hundred and eight nine Americans are buried here, the vast majority from the US 3rd (George Patton) and 7th (Alexander Patch) Armies operations in 1944/45. It is the second largest cemetery of American dead in Europe, after the World War One cemetery at Meuse-Argonne, which contains fourteen thousand two hundred and forty six Americans.
Visiting the grave of Mr Kreiling's best friend, Donald A Brinkerhoff, who was killed in action on 29 March 1945. It is probable that he was killed fighting in Weinheim, Germany, in what the regimental diary Mr Kreiling is holding notes was very vicious fighting against a hodgepodge of old men, children, and seasoned Eastern Front veterans.
The front-cover interior of the book Mr Kreiling is holding in the above picture - an essential piece of the puzzle in finding out where these pictures were taken. Mr and Mrs Kreiling were married for 62 years until his death in 2008. Mrs Kreiling passed away in 2010. The book "With the 114th in the ETO", for those who also fancy giving it a read. https://digitalcollections.nypl.org/items/7cde3200-18f0-0137-8a22-27bc73dace33 This is just a small selection of the images of their journey, very few of which were published, the best source of them - not particularly user-friendly! - is this, followed by clicking through the 'related images': http://images.google.com/hosted/life/62e214561a6db980.html
This post is dedicated to the memory of Mr and Mrs Kreiling; and to all those who did their part in the defeat of Nazism. Particularly those who gave their tomorrows for our todays.
item.dc:creator: BodicaRexVII

item.title: I don't have a girlfriend so here's my 1973 pocket calculator (featuring 1000FPS slomo)
item.description:
Texas Instruments Datamath. Effectively the first pocket calculator.
50 year old peelies
Pi² at 1000fps on the nixie tube LED screen. What you are watching is the logic actually flowing through logic gates until it flows through them all and settles on the answer.
Pi² at real time speed. I used to think calculators had a chip that stored all the answers on it so I used to try to come up with math questions I didn't think they would expect.
Valentine's Day tax
item.dc:creator: BaklavaWearingBalaclavaWhilePlayingBalalaikaOnBlackLava
